summary: Dan Luu analyzes whether running ads on his blog makes financial sense, ultimately concluding it probably doesn't despite potential revenue of $600-2,300/month. He examines the tradeoffs between modest ad revenue (which would never approach his tech salary) versus downsides like reduced traffic, privacy concerns, perverse incentives, and hassle. Through updates spanning two years, he discovers his traffic grew enough to theoretically support living expenses, yet still chooses not to run ads due to the hassle factor, comparing this to developers who rationally leave money on the table by choosing startups over higher-paying big tech jobs.
key_points: At typical CPM rates, even successful programming blogs generate modest income - Jeff Atwood's Coding Horror at peak traffic would earn only ~$1,700/month, far below tech salaries
Ads impose hidden costs: reduced traffic, privacy concerns for readers, unconscious bias toward optimizing for revenue rather than quality content
The ratio of traffic to Alexa rankings is wildly inaccurate - Coding Horror ranked 7x higher despite having 1/4 the traffic over the measured period
After two years, traffic grew to 2.3M hits/month (potentially $100k/year with optimization), yet running ads still felt like too much hassle
About 40% of readers use ad blockers, and top programmer crowdfunding campaigns earn an order of magnitude less than potential ad revenue
money_quotes: The wildly optimistic success rate is that you barely cover rent when the programming job market is hot enough that mid-level positions at big companies pay out total compensation that's 8x-9x the median income in the U.S. That's not good.
It seems totally irrational to leave $90k/yr on the table because 'it seems like a hassle', but here we are.

I regularly talk to people who leave $200k/yr or more on the table by working for startups instead of large companies, and that seems like a reasonable preference to me. They make 'enough' money and like things the way they are. So why can't not running ads be a reasonable preference?

My impression is that people are mostly concerned with really big companies that have enough information that they could deanonymize people if they were so inclined, like Google and Facebook, but some people are probably also concerned about smaller ad networks like Carbon.

People are influenced by money, even when they don't notice it. I'm people. I might do something to get more revenue, even though the dollar amount is small and I wouldn't consciously spend a lot of effort of optimizing things to get an extra $5/month.
key_insight: Even when blog monetization becomes theoretically lucrative enough to support living expenses, the rational choice for someone with tech industry earning power may be to leave that money on the table if the hassle and tradeoffs don't align with personal preferences - just as developers rationally choose lower-paying jobs they prefer.
